turns.raw_text: And yet not so well as with a pruning-hook made for the purpose?

turns.speaker: thrasymachus
turns.raw_text: May we not say that this is the end of a pruning-hook?

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: Then now I think you will have no difficulty in understanding my meaning when the question whether the end of anything would be that which could not be accomplished, or not so well accomplished, by any other thing?

turns.speaker: thrasymachus
turns.raw_text: I understand your meaning and assent.

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: And that to which an end is appointed has also an excellence? Need I ask again whether the eye has an end?

turns.speaker: thrasymachus
turns.raw_text: And has not the eye an excellence?

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: And the ear has an end and an excellence also?

metadata.notes: Thrasymachus dialogue: justice as the advantage of the stronger

turns.speaker: thrasymachus
turns.raw_text: And the same is true of all other things; they have each of them an end and a special excellence?

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: That is so.

turns.speaker: thrasymachus
turns.raw_text: Well, and can the eyes fulfil their end if they are wanting in their own proper excellence and have a defect instead? How can they if they are blind and cannot see?

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: You mean to say, if they have lost their proper excellence, which is sight; but I have not arrived at that point yet. I would rather ask the question more generally, and only enquire whether the things which fulfil their ends fulfil them by their own proper excellence, and fail of fulfilling them by their own defect?

turns.speaker: thrasymachus
turns.raw_text: I might say the same of the ears; when deprived of their own proper excellence they cannot fulfil their end?

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: And the same observation will apply to all other things?

turns.speaker: thrasymachus
turns.raw_text: Well; and has not the soul an end which nothing else can fulfil? for example, to superintend and command and deliberate and the like. Are not these functions proper to the soul, and can they rightly be assigned to any other?

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: To no other.

turns.speaker: thrasymachus
turns.raw_text: And is not life to be reckoned among the ends of the soul?

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: And has not the soul an excellence also?

metadata.notes: Thrasymachus dialogue: justice as the advantage of the stronger

turns.speaker: thrasymachus
turns.raw_text: And can she or can she not fulfil her own ends when deprived of that excellence?

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: She cannot.

turns.speaker: thrasymachus
turns.raw_text: Then an evil soul must necessarily be an evil ruler and superintendent, and the good soul a good ruler?

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: Yes, necessarily.

turns.speaker: thrasymachus
turns.raw_text: And we have admitted that justice is the excellence of the soul, and injustice the defect of the soul?

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: That has been admitted.

turns.speaker: thrasymachus
turns.raw_text: Then the just soul and the just man will live well, and the unjust man will live ill?

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: That is what your argument proves.

turns.speaker: thrasymachus
turns.raw_text: And he who lives well is blessed and happy, and he who lives ill the reverse of happy?

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: Certainly.

metadata.notes: Thrasymachus dialogue: justice as the advantage of the stronger

turns.speaker: thrasymachus
turns.raw_text: Then the just is happy, and the unjust miserable?

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: But happiness and not misery is profitable.

turns.speaker: thrasymachus
turns.raw_text: Of course.

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: Then, my blessed Thrasymachus, injustice can never be more profitable than justice.

turns.speaker: thrasymachus
turns.raw_text: Let this, Socrates be your entertainment at the Bendidea.

turns.speaker: socrates
turns.raw_text: For which I am indebted to you now that you have grown gentle towards me and have left off scolding. Nevertheless, I have not been well entertained; but that was my own fault and not yours. As an epicure snatches a taste of every dish which is successively brought to table, he not having allowed himself time to enjoy the one before, so have I gone from one subject to another without having discovered what I sought at first, the nature of justice. I left that enquiry and turned away to consider whether justice is virtue and wisdom or evil and folly; and when there arose a further question about the comparative advantages of justice and injustice, I could not refrain from passing on to that. And the result of the whole discussion has been that I know nothing at all. For I know not what justice is, and therefore I am not likely to know whether it is or is not a virtue, nor can I say whether the just man is happy or unhappy.

metadata.notes: Thrasymachus dialogue: justice as the advantage of the stronger
